note_data.title: Infectious disease
keyPoints: **Pathogens** are disease-causing organisms including bacteria, viruses, fungi and protoctists that invade host tissues
**Primary defences** prevent pathogen entry and include skin as a barrier and stomach acid that destroys pathogens
**Phagocytosis** involves white blood cells engulfing pathogens and destroying them using lysosomes
**Lymphocytes** produce specific antibodies complementary to antigens on pathogen surfaces
**Antibodies** are Y-shaped proteins that bind to specific antigens causing agglutination and neutralisation
**Active immunity** develops when lymphocytes produce antibodies and memory cells after antigen exposure
**Vaccination** introduces antigens to stimulate antibody production and memory cell formation without causing disease
**HIV** attacks helper T lymphocytes reducing the immune response leading to AIDS
**Memory cells** remain in blood after infection and enable rapid secondary immune response upon re-exposure
**Passive immunity** provides temporary protection through antibodies from another organism

definitions.definition: A disease-causing organism that invades and damages host tissues
A Y-shaped protein produced by lymphocytes that is complementary to and binds with specific antigens
Immunity developed when lymphocytes are stimulated to produce antibodies and memory cells
The introduction of antigens into the body to stimulate an immune response and memory cell formation
The process by which white blood cells engulf and destroy pathogens using lysosomes

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or detailed account of the process
State similarities and differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain how vaccination provides protection against infectious disease. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Vaccination introduces antigens from pathogens into the body. These antigens stimulate lymphocytes to produce specific antibodies complementary to the antigens. Memory cells are formed and remain in the blood. If the same pathogen enters later, memory cells rapidly produce large quantities of antibodies providing faster secondary immune response.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Lymphocytes stimulated to produce antibodies
Mark 4: Rapid secondary response upon re-infection

commonMistakes: Never say antibodies kill pathogens — antibodies bind to antigens causing agglutination
Students confuse active and passive immunity — active involves producing your own antibodies
Don't say vaccines contain weakened pathogens — they contain antigens from pathogens
Never say white blood cells produce antibodies — only lymphocytes produce antibodies
Students forget that HIV specifically attacks helper T lymphocytes, not all white blood cells
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2
Always distinguish between primary and secondary immune responses
Learn the specific sequence: antigen recognition → lymphocyte activation → antibody production → memory cell formation
HIV questions often link to reduced immunity and opportunistic infections
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Structure of an antibody showing Y-shape with variable regions that bind to specific antigens, and constant regions. Also draw phagocytosis sequence showing engulfment and lysosome fusion.
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how white blood cells defend the body against bacterial infection, requiring detailed description of phagocytosis and antibody production
O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — tested knowledge of which type of pathogen causes malaria, with common incorrect answers being bacteria or virus instead of protozoa
M/J 2022 P2 Q7 (5 marks) — required students to describe the body's barriers to infection and explain why antibiotics are ineffective against viral diseases
O/N 2021 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — asked for comparison between active and passive immunity, including examples and duration of protection

workedExample.question: Explain how vaccination provides protection against infectious disease. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that vaccination involves introducing antigens (dead/weakened pathogens or their toxins)
Step 2: Explain primary immune response - white blood cells recognize antigens as foreign
Step 3: Describe antibody production by lymphocytes specific to the antigen
Step 4: Explain memory cell formation during primary response
Step 5: Describe secondary response when real pathogen enters - memory cells rapidly produce antibodies
Step 6: State this provides active immunity with long-lasting protection
workedExample.answer: Vaccination introduces antigens from dead, weakened or modified pathogens into the body. White blood cells recognize these antigens as foreign and mount a primary immune response. Specific lymphocytes produce antibodies complementary to the antigens. Memory cells are formed during this response. When the actual pathogen later infects the body, memory cells quickly recognize the antigens and rapidly produce large quantities of specific antibodies in a secondary immune response. This destroys the pathogen before disease symptoms develop, providing active immunity that lasts for many years.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: antigens introduced, primary immune response, antibody production, memory cell formation, rapid secondary response, active immunity mentioned

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused antibiotics with antibodies' — always distinguish that antibiotics are drugs that kill bacteria, while antibodies are proteins made by white blood cells

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently failed to explain why antibiotics don't work against viruses' — always state that viruses reproduce inside host cells and antibiotics cannot penetrate these cells

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often mixed up active and passive immunity' — remember active immunity involves making your own antibodies (vaccination/infection), passive immunity receives ready-made antibodies (breast milk/injection)
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw and label the process of phagocytosis: (1) White blood cell detects pathogen using chemical signals, (2) Cell membrane extends around pathogen forming pseudopodia, (3) Pathogen enclosed in food vacuole inside cytoplasm, (4) Digestive enzymes released into vacuole, (5) Pathogen broken down and digested, (6) Waste materials removed from cell. Include clear labels for white blood cell, pathogen, cell membrane, pseudopodia, food vacuole, and digestive enzymes.
note_data.quickRecap: Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa) that enter through broken barriers. The body defends using white blood cells through phagocytosis and antibody production. Vaccination provides active immunity by introducing antigens to create memory cells. Antibiotics only work against bacteria, not viruses, because viruses reproduce inside host cells.
